meta.title: L560 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Range Rover Velar L560 engine — Ingenium diesel/petrol, D300, P400, SVAutobiography V8. Real UK costs and reliability data. Get free quotes from 12 vetted Land Rover specialists today.

@graph.name: Range Rover Velar L560 Engine Codes
Range Rover Velar L560 Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover Velar L560 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Velar engine typically costs £3,000–£9,500 supply only [EM-QUOTE], depending on the engine — the four-cylinder Ingeniums sit at £3,000–£5,800, the D300 V6 diesel at £6,500–£9,500, and the SVAutobiography V8 at £5,500–£9,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Velar L560 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 204PT P300 (Ingenium petrol) and the 306PS P400 (3.0 mild-hybrid petrol) are the most reliable options — both are free of the diesel timing chain issues and the pre-facelift AJ126 cooling problems. The P400 is the sweet spot: modern, efficient, and proven.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Velar L560?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre-2019 204DTD Ingenium diesels suffer from timing chain tensioner wear. The D300 V6 diesel (306DT) has a known crankshaft bearing risk — short oil intervals are critical. The AJ126 V6 petrol (pre-facelift P340/P380) suffers from water pump and cooling system leaks. The V8 is robust but expensive to service.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Velar L560 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and year. The petrol Ingeniums (204PT, 306PS) are the most reliable. The D300 V6 diesel is powerful but requires obsessive maintenance — 5,000-mile oil changes are essential. Pre-2019 four-cylinder diesels carry a timing chain risk that's well-documented. Post-2019 diesel Ingeniums are improved.

mainEntity.name: Which Velar L560 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre-2019 204DTD diesel without documented timing chain tensioner replacement — it's the same risk as earlier Ingenium units. A neglected D300 without oil change history is also high-risk. The AJ126 V6 petrol is fine but check for cooling system leaks. Avoid any engine without full service history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Velar L560 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (Ingenium chain), cold-start knock (D300 bearing), persistent coolant loss (V6 petrol), or limp mode (sensor/injector faults) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Velar L560 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a £20,000 D300, a £6,500–£9,500 recon is 33–48% of the car's value — well within our 60% Land Rover threshold. Against a high-mileage £15,000 example, the same recon is 43–63% — borderline. For a P400 at £35,000, a £4,500–£7,500 recon is 13–21% — a straightforward repair decision. Value the car first; if it's a pre-2019 diesel without chain history, evaluate carefully.

mainEntity.name: How long do Velar L560 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 204PT and 306PS petrols can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing. The D300 is capable of high mileage but requires 5,000-mile oil changes to mitigate bearing risk — many specialists recommend treating 100,000 miles as a major service checkpoint. The AJ126 V6 petrol is robust but cooling system maintenance is critical.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the pre-facelift and facelift Velar L560?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both share the L560 chassis code. The 2020 facelift introduced the mild-hybrid 3.0 P400 (306PS) to replace the AJ126 P340/P380 V6 petrol. The diesel range remained largely unchanged, though post-2019 units received improved timing chain tensioners. Costs and reliability differ accordingly — the P400 is the better petrol choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Velar L560 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. For the diesel, look for 204DTD (2.0) or 306DT (3.0). For petrol, it's 204PT (2.0), AJ126 (3.0 pre-facelift), 306PS (3.0 facelift), or AJ133 (5.0 V8). Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it — 12 vetted Land Rover specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Range Rover Velar L560 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Velar L560 — from the four-cylinder Ingenium diesel and petrol to the six-cylinder D300 and the 550bhp supercharged V8 in the SVAutobiography. Real UK replacement costs and honest reliability data for Range Rover's most design-led SUV. Backed by 155+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Range Rover Velar L560 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Velar L560 — including the Ingenium fours, the V6 diesel and petrol units, and the supercharged V8 that tops the range.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any Velar engine family. The 204PT (Ingenium petrol) and 306PS (V6 petrol) carry the strongest reliability reputations based on specialist consensus. The 306DT (D300) is known for crankshaft bearing risk, a pattern shared with the wider 3.0 V6 diesel family. The AJ133 V8 is robust but timing chain tensioner wear is a pre-2013 risk — Velar's later units are improved. The 204DTD (Ingenium diesel) carries pre-2019 tensioner risk.

overview.intro: The Velar L560 was launched in 2017 as Range Rover's fourth model line, designed to fill the gap between the Evoque and the Range Rover Sport. Named after the original Range Rover prototypes from 1969, it brought a new design language — hidden door handles, a minimalist cabin, and a sweeping roofline — to the brand. Built on JLR's D7a aluminium-intensive platform, the Velar shares its engines with the F-Pace and Discovery Sport, including four-cylinder Ingenium diesels and petrols, V6 diesels, and — for the SVAutobiography — a supercharged 5.0-litre V8. A 2020 facelift introduced the mild-hybrid 3.0 P400 petrol.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: JLR D7a (aluminium-intensive, longitudinal engine, four-wheel drive) • Predecessor: None — Velar is a new model line • Successor: None (L560 is the current generation) • Sibling body styles in this generation: Single body style (five-door SUV)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The Velar L560 generated 155 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], with the D300 V6 diesel accounting for 120 of those — the majority of Velar demand is for the six-cylinder diesel.

items.quote: The six-cylinder diesel delivers 296bhp and 700Nm of torque — effortless towing and motorway cruising. The bearing risk is real, but properly maintained examples (5k oil intervals) are robust.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who need six-cylinder torque for towing or motorway miles.

items.quote: The later Ingenium-based 3.0 with mild hybrid is smoother, more efficient, and — critically — free of the AJ126 cooling system issues that affect earlier cars. The P400 is the sweet spot in the petrol range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want performance without V8 running costs.

items.quote: Timing chain tensioner wear on pre-2019 diesels remains the same risk as earlier Ingenium units — cold-start rattle is the warning, and a snapped chain means full replacement. Post-2019 units are improved.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers with documented tensioner replacement history.

items.quote: Supercharger and timing chain work on the 5.0 V8 is eye-wateringly expensive — a full engine replacement can exceed £9,000. The Velar's V8 is rare, making parts harder to source.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want a supercharged V8 SUV.

items.quote: The four-cylinder petrol Ingenium is more reliable than its diesel sibling, cheaper to repair, and delivers 296bhp — enough for most buyers. No timing chain tensioner risk, no DPF.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want Velar refinement without diesel or V8 risk.

items.quote: The earlier V6 petrol is smooth, sounds great, and is actually cheaper to buy than the P400 on the used market. Watch for water pumps and cooling system leaks, but otherwise robust.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a naturally-aspirated-feel V6 without the P400's mild-hybrid complexity.

ownershipEconomics.h2: Range Rover Velar L560 — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Pre-2019 Risk — budget for chain history
High Power, High Risk — 5k oil intervals essential
Pre-Facelift Petrol — sounds great, watch cooling
Best Petrol V6 — the one to buy
Rare & Costly — for collectors and enthusiasts

economicsRule.title: The L560 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,500–£9,500 D300 recon against a £20,000–£55,000 Velar sits at 12–48% of the car's value — well within our 60% Land Rover scrap threshold. Against a high-mileage £18,000 D300, the same recon is 36–53% — still viable but at the upper end. Value the car first; if the D300 has no documented oil history, walk away. The P400 recon at £4,500–£7,500 against a £35,000 car is 13–21% — a repair decision, not a scrap question.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L560 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 204PT P300 — four-cylinder petrol, no DPF, cheaper repairs than the diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L560 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: AJ133 V8 — supercharger and timing chain work is expensive; parts are rare.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L560 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 306DT D300 — the V6 diesel dominates UK demand; just budget for 5k oil intervals.

problems.title: 204DTD Ingenium Diesel Timing Chain Tensioner
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately — a £1,200–£2,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,500–£5,800 replacement that follows if ignored. On a Velar worth under £18,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

problems.title: 306DT D300 Crankshaft Bearing Failure
problems.recommendation: If your D300 shows any sign of cold-start knock, investigate immediately — £5,500–£7,000 for a bottom-end rebuild is cheaper than the £6,500–£9,500 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. On a Velar worth under £20,000, evaluate the car's overall condition carefully before committing.

problems.title: AJ126 3.0 V6 Petrol Cooling System Leaks
problems.recommendation: Have the cooling system inspected at every service — a £600–£1,200 preventative water pump and crossover pipe replacement is far cheaper than the £4,500+ replacement that follows an overheat. On a Velar worth over £25,000, this is a straightforward repair decision.

problems.title: AJ133 5.0 V8 Timing Chain Tensioner (SVAutobiography)
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a Velar SVAutobiography, look for documented chain tensioner history — a £2,500–£4,500 preventative job is cheaper than the £5,500+ replacement that follows a failure. On a £40,000+ example, this is a manageable cost of ownership.

replacementCosts.h2: Velar L560 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are Velar L560 only. The P400 uses the later Ingenium-based 3.0 with mild hybrid (2020–present), while the P340/P380 use the earlier AJ126 V6 (2017–2020). The SVAutobiography uses the supercharged 5.0 AJ133 V8.
